rescue_reasoning: Climate screen is explicit_self. The verbatim text includes an explicit mitigation outcome via energy demand reduction: 'cutting electricity consumption by 30% or more' through 'variable speed devices and high efficiency equipment' and potentially 'establishing a SCADA... system for monitoring and control'. This matches the canonical mitigation mechanism (water/wastewater energy efficiency upgrade → energy_use_reduction). The gate failure is due to missing a specific mitigation token, but the source clearly states reduced electricity consumption, which is a valid mitigation outcome.
policy_statement: For water delivery, Governorate services will conduct a detailed assessment of the water distribution network and wastewater treatment to identify energy-saving investments (e.g., variable speed pumps, high-efficiency equipment) and consider establishing a SCADA monitoring and control system.
mechanism_description: Governorate services plan to assess water distribution and wastewater treatment operations and pursue efficiency upgrades (e.g., variable speed pumps, high-efficiency equipment) and possibly SCADA monitoring/control to cut electricity consumption.
primary_causal_pathway: Same water/wastewater service → ↓ energy input (electricity/fuels) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Utilities/governorate operators retrofit and optimize pumps, aeration, motors, and process equipment (e.g., variable speed drives, high-efficiency units) and add monitoring/control (e.g., SCADA) to run systems closer to optimal setpoints and schedules.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Improved electromechanical and process efficiency reduces kWh set per m³ delivered/treated by lowering friction/overpumping, improving pump curve matching, reducing unnecessary head/pressure, and optimizing treatment energy (e.g., aeration control).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower energy demand reduces combustion and grid-generation emissions (CO₂e); in some cases also reduces peak demand and upstream fuel use.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced electricity use in water and wastewater operations, leading to lower GHG emissions from energy supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: efficiency upgrades in water/wastewater operations reduce energy consumption, which reduces GHG emissions—this is a mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although water-sector measures can support adaptation (service continuity under heat/drought) or resource efficiency (water loss reduction), the mechanism as stated is explicitly energy-efficiency/energy-use reduction as the main lever, making emissions reduction the dominant climate effect.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy instance focuses mainly on leak reduction or demand reduction (water saved) with energy savings as secondary, it could trend toward Resource Efficiency; if focused on resilience/backup power, it could trend toward Adaptation. Those are not the defining features of this mechanism.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that upgrades water/wastewater equipment/controls to cut electricity use will, in general, primarily reduce energy-related emissions, regardless of location or grid mix (magnitude varies, direction consistent).  
5) Note on secondary subtype: Any secondary categorization (e.g., also reducing non-revenue water, improving reliability, enabling better methane management) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on explicit evidence, and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If upgrades increase treatment intensity (e.g., higher-pressure operation or added treatment stages) energy use could rise despite efficiency measures; conversely, SCADA may primarily be installed for reliability/leak management rather than energy. Classification should be revisited only if a policy’s stated primary intent/effect is not energy reduction.
key_indicators: “conduct a detailed assessment”; “identify energy-saving investments”; “variable speed devices”; “high efficiency equipment”; “cutting electricity consumption by 30% or more”; “establishing a SCADA … system for monitoring and control”
co_benefits: Improved service reliability/continuity, reduced operating costs, better leak/pressure management, improved wastewater treatment performance and compliance.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be coded as infrastructure-investment if the policy were framed as committing funds/implementation for pumps/SCADA; here it is primarily an assessment to identify/consider investments, so planning-strategy with enabling directness fits best.
